title: Charge-Conjugation Violation and Population Asymmetry in Bipartite Fermionic Lattices
authors: Di Xiao, Xue-Ting Fang, Lushuai Cao, Zhong-Kun Hu, Peter Schmelcher
abstract: Charge conjugation violation (CCV) is a central concept in particle physics and appears also for quasiparticles in quantum many-body systems, which typically relies on an embedded external symmetry breaking to the underlying system. An open question is how an intrinsic CCV mechanism could emerge and what its macroscopic consequences would be. We establish sublattice kinks in bipartite fermionic lattices as a concrete setup showing intrinsic CCV. The intrinsic CCV of the sublattice kink is based on the graph-topological nature of the underlying Hamiltonian, with no explicit symmetry breaking taking place. It leads to a population asymmetry of different configurations and imprints a hidden leaf-like structure in the eigenenergy spectrum. The population asymmetry also leads to an imbalanced sublattice-kink production triggered by the vacuum-instability in the quench dynamics. Our work demonstrates the graph topology as the microscopic origin of intrinsic CCV, with the population asymmetry as the macroscopic consequence, of which the proposed setup is highly amenable to experimental implementation via cold-atom quantum simulators.
summary: The paper establishes that topological features within bipartite fermionic lattices can induce intrinsic Charge-Conjugation Violation (CCV) without external symmetry breaking. This microscopic mechanism manifests macroscopically as a population imbalance between particle and anti-particle excitations. The findings are highly relevant for experimental realization using cold-atom quantum simulators.
main_problem: The study investigates how intrinsic Charge-Conjugation Violation (CCV) can emerge in quantum many-body systems without relying on explicit symmetry breaking mechanisms.
main_result: Intrinsic CCV is shown to arise from the graph topology of bipartite fermionic lattices, leading macroscopically to a measurable population asymmetry between kink and anti-kink excitations.
method: The authors use an effective Hamiltonian approach derived from mapping the system onto sublattice kinks (topological defects) and analyzing its spectral properties under quench dynamics.
model_or_system: The model is based on bipartite fermionic lattices containing quasiparticles called 'sublattice kinks' (SKs), governed by a Fermi-Hubbard type Hamiltonian restricted to specific Hilbert subspaces.
key_observables: Population asymmetry ($\delta n = n_K - n_{ ar{K}}$), hidden leaf-like structure in the eigenenergy spectrum, and imbalanced sublattice-kink production during quench dynamics.
important_parameters: Chemical potentials ($\mu_K$, $\mu_{ ar{K}}$) and external boundary tilts ($h$) used to tune the asymmetry; lattice geometry (bipartite vs. ladder).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mapping the system dynamics to an effective kink picture, sometimes requiring truncation of the Hilbert space or assuming non-interacting spinless fermions in certain limits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematics of CCV in SKs (Fig. 1), show the leaf-like structure in the eigenenergy spectrum related to asymmetry (Fig. 2), and demonstrate control over K-$ ar{K}$ imbalance via bi-quench protocols (Fig. 3).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general problem of intrinsic CCV, developing the model using kink excitations on bipartite lattices, demonstrating measurable asymmetry in static spectra and quench dynamics, and finally extending the analysis to include doublon/holon physics.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses emergent symmetry breaking mechanisms in lattice models, which is highly relevant for understanding non-equilibrium phenomena in ultracold atomic quantum simulators used to model strongly correlated systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematics of SKs with the CCV and kink- number violation. (a) the transformation between the bipartite fermionic lattice (upper panel) and the SK bond (lower panel). (b1,b2) differentiate between the SK-vacuum states |vac:L⟩kink (b1) and |vac:R⟩kink (b2) by the virtual SK, and (b3) the virtual-real bond cou- pling. (c) The CCV in terms of the K −¯K chemical potential asymmetry shown in both the underlying bi- partite lattice and the SK picture. (d) The first and second rows illustrate a representative kink configura- tion and the H-graph, respectively, for the CSO ladder lattice (left) and the bipartite lattice (right).

(b) The leaf-like structures shown in light of the intra-cell correlation, with the color and marker type indicating the 𝛿𝑛and 𝑛¯K, respectively, of each eigenstate.
